children.name: Lacerta
children.description: True lizards of open habitats. The Sand Lizard is one of only two lizard species breeding in Sweden — males develop vivid green flanks in breeding season, transforming from drab brown to brilliant green over several weeks in spring. It requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying and is declining across its northern range.

children.name: Lacerta agilis
children.description: One of two lizard species native to Sweden; breeding males develop vivid green flanks; requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying; declining in northern range; Swedish populations are protected.

children.name: Lacerta viridis
children.description: Brilliant iridescent green; males have a blue throat in breeding season; largest lacertid in Europe at up to 40 cm.

children.name: Lacerta bilineata
children.description: Recently split from L. viridis; France and Iberia.

children.name: Lacerta strigata
children.description: A slender green lizard from the Caucasus, Iran and Turkmenistan; males display bright blue throats in spring.

children.name: Lacerta media
children.description: A Middle Eastern species found in Iran, Iraq and the Levant; prefers oak woodlands and rocky slopes.

children.name: Lacerta pamphylica
children.description: Restricted to the Mediterranean coast of Turkey; isolated population of green lizards with distinctive dorsal spotting.

children.name: Lacerta anatolica
children.description: Endemic to western Anatolia; inhabits maquis shrubland and rocky hillsides.

children.name: Lacerta cypernica
children.description: Endemic to Cyprus; the largest green lizard on the island; prefers forest edges.

children.name: Lacerta trilineata
children.description: A large green lizard of the Balkans; three yellow lines run the length of its body.

children.name: Zootoca
children.description: Cold-adapted viviparous lizards.

children.name: Zootoca vivipara
children.description: Sweden's most widespread reptile; found from southern Sweden to the Arctic fell; viviparous in most populations — gives birth to live young rather than laying eggs; a critical adaptation to cold northern climates.

children.name: Zootoca carniolica
children.description: A recently split species from the Z. vivipara complex; restricted to the Dinaric Alps of Slovenia and Croatia; lays eggs unlike northern populations.

children.name: Zootoca louisanti
children.description: A microendemic of the Pyrenees; separated from Z. vivipara by genetic analysis and ecological niche differences.

children.name: Zootoca monticola
children.description: A high-elevation form from the Alps; bears live young at altitudes above 2000 m.

children.name: Zootoca horvathi
children.description: A secretive species from the Dinaric karst; prefers limestone rock crevices.
